title: The relational self: An interpersonal social-cognitive theory
research_question: How can a theory of self and personality explain both stable interpersonal patterns and variation across relationships?
method: Theoretical integration of experimental social-cognitive research on transference, significant-other representations, self-evaluation, motivation, and self-regulation.
population_or_data: Published experimental evidence on significant-other representations and transference; the article does not report a new participant sample.
core_finding: Knowledge about significant others is linked in memory with relationship-specific self-knowledge; cues that activate a significant-other representation can evoke the corresponding relational self, while chronic accessibility helps produce recurring patterns.
architecture_implications: Represent self-knowledge as a repertoire of relationship-scoped schemas rather than one global persona, with explicit links between a person or relationship cue and the self-state it may activate.
Treat relationship-conditioned retrieval as probabilistic: preserve a stable default while recording which contextual cues supported each relational-self hypothesis and how certain that linkage is.
limitations: This is a theory-building review rather than a new representative study. The experiments it integrates use constrained transference paradigms, so the account does not establish how completely relational selves explain natural longitudinal messaging or culturally diverse relationships.

Andersen Chen 2002 relational self DOI 10.1037

title: Social Influence in Computer-Mediated Communication: The Effects of Anonymity on Group Behavior
research_question: When does visual anonymity in computer-mediated groups increase conformity to a salient group norm?
method: Two controlled computer-mediated group experiments manipulated identifiability and primed either efficiency or prosocial behavior, then examined task behavior and transmission of the primed norm.
population_or_data: Participants in two short-term experimental computer-mediated groups; sample sizes are not reported in the inspected abstract.
core_finding: Anonymous groups produced solutions consistent with the primed norm, whereas identifiable groups did not; in the second study, unprimed members followed primed members only under anonymity, indicating norm transmission when group identity was salient.
architecture_implications: Store active group identity and local group norms as contextual inputs to behavior generation; absence of individual identity cues must not be treated as absence of social influence.
Keep group-conditioned behavior separate from durable owner traits so a norm expressed in one group does not silently become a global preference or value.
limitations: The evidence comes from brief laboratory groups, explicit primes, and constructed tasks. It does not show that anonymity has the same effect in established relationships or private messaging, and it cannot identify a persistent personal disposition.

title: Between You and Me: Setting Work-Nonwork Boundaries in the Context of Workplace Relationships
research_question: How do workplace relationships shape the way people negotiate boundaries between work and nonwork roles?
method: Inductive qualitative study of boundary-work episodes and outcomes in a large United States law firm.
population_or_data: Seventy attorneys in one large United States law firm.
core_finding: Attorneys used different boundary tactics in different relationships; relationship type activated approach or avoidance motives, and the combination of relationship and tactic shaped both boundary-setting success and relational consequences.
architecture_implications: Model roles and information boundaries per relationship, including the substantive boundary goal and the relational cost of enforcing it, instead of storing a single work-versus-personal preference.

Represent boundary negotiation as an event with counterpart, tactic, outcome, and role context so later behavior can distinguish a general preference from an accommodation made for a particular person.
limitations: The study is qualitative and confined to attorneys in one large United States firm. Work-nonwork boundaries may not transfer directly to family, friendship, or cross-cultural messaging, and the design does not estimate tactic prevalence.

Trefalt 2013 relationship boundary work 70 attorneys DOI

title: Language style as audience design
research_question: Can within-speaker style variation be explained as a response to present and reference audiences?
method: A sociolinguistic theory paper synthesizing evidence on style shifting, accommodation, code choice, and mass communication.
population_or_data: Sociolinguistic cases and prior empirical work involving broadcast speech and bilingual or bidialectal variation; no single participant sample is reported in the inspected abstract.
core_finding: Speakers orient most strongly to direct addressees, but auditors, overhearers, and absent reference groups can also shape style; some shifts respond to a changed audience while others proactively redefine the situation.
architecture_implications: Use an audience model that distinguishes addressee, auditor, overhearer, and reference group, and condition lexical, register, and code choices on those roles.
Allow both responsive style shifts and deliberate identity-signaling shifts, but retain provenance so stylistic divergence from an addressee is not misread as inconsistency.
limitations: The article is a foundational theoretical account centered on sociolinguistic and mass-communication cases before contemporary social platforms. It does not test individualized chat generation, algorithmic audience selection, or private multi-party messaging.

Bell audience design DOI S004740450001037X

title: Children's linguistic choices: Audience design and societal norms
research_question: Does Bell's audience hierarchy adequately explain children's style variation in a multilingual social environment?
method: Longitudinal analysis of Trinidad Creole and Standard English verb-form variation across changing social situations.
population_or_data: Three preschool children in Trinidad observed longitudinally.
core_finding: All three children varied style, but speech changed even when audience factors were stable; broader social and educational demands and the personal significance of particular people complicated a fixed addressee-to-eavesdropper hierarchy.
architecture_implications: Do not route style from audience role alone: include domain norms, institutional expectations, cultural context, and the personal significance of each audience member.
Represent audience effects as learned, revisable weights rather than a universal hierarchy, and preserve counterexamples where the same audience co-occurs with different styles.
limitations: The longitudinal evidence is rich but comes from only three preschool children in one specific sociolinguistic setting. Developmental stage and local Creole-English norms sharply limit generalization to adults or text messaging.

search_queries: site:cambridge.org "Children's linguistic choices: Audience design and societal norms"
Youssef 1993 audience design societal norms DOI

title: Speakers' experiences and audience design: knowing when and knowing how to adjust utterances to addressees
research_question: What interaction experience enables speakers to notice when audience design is needed and to produce an appropriate adjustment?
method: Controlled referential-communication experiment in which Directors described picture-card arrays for two Matchers with different prior knowledge and later switched partners.
population_or_data: Directors and two independent Matchers in a laboratory picture-card task; the inspected abstract does not report the participant count.
core_finding: Directors generally distinguished shared from unshared items, but adaptation improved at the second partner change, consistent with speakers learning both that adjustment was needed and what form it should take in that setting.
architecture_implications: Treat audience adaptation as an experience-dependent capability, not a guaranteed consequence of knowing the recipient; store evidence about which adjustments worked for each partner and task.
When partner knowledge is uncertain, prefer explicit clarification or fuller references over assuming the model can automatically overcome habitual brevity and wording consistency.
limitations: The study uses a tightly controlled picture-description task and engineered partner changes; sample size is not available in the inspected abstract. It does not establish how audience learning operates in long-running, emotionally meaningful text relationships.

search_queries: site:sciencedirect.com "Speakers' experiences and audience design"
Horton Gerrig 2002 audience design DOI

title: Conceptual pacts and lexical choice in conversation
venue: Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition
research_question: Does repeated lexical convergence arise only from salience and recency, or from partner-specific shared conceptualizations?
method: Three laboratory referential-communication experiments tested historical versus ahistorical explanations of repeated object naming.
population_or_data: Participants in three controlled conversation experiments; sample sizes are not reported in the inspected PubMed abstract.
core_finding: Speakers developed shared ways of conceptualizing objects and continued using those expressions even when shorter labels were available; the pacts simplified over time and could be replaced when needed.
architecture_implications: Store conversational aliases and shared conceptualizations with partner and thread scope, including the evidence and time period in which each pact was established.
Prefer an established partner-specific term during retrieval, but allow explicit renegotiation and retain prior aliases so changed wording does not erase shared history.
limitations: The evidence comes from short laboratory object-naming tasks rather than natural relationships or text dialogue. Repetition, task structure, and shared visual context may account for effects that will be weaker in open-ended conversation.

search_queries: site:pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8921603 "Conceptual pacts"
Brennan Clark 1996 conceptual pacts DOI

title: When conceptual pacts are broken: Partner-specific effects on the comprehension of referring expressions
research_question: Do listeners encode an established referring expression as specific to the conversational partner who helped create it?
method: A participant and confederate completed a grid-based matching task; the original or a new speaker later used either the established expression or a new expression for the same object, and comprehension latency was measured.
population_or_data: Naive participants paired with an experimental confederate in a referential matching task; the inspected publisher material does not state the sample size.
core_finding: A changed expression delayed identification when it came from the original partner but not when it came from a new partner, consistent with listeners retaining a partner-specific expectation about established wording.
architecture_implications: Index lexical and conceptual pacts by partner identity; a term change from the same partner can warrant a higher-priority meaning-change check than the same change from a new partner, while preserving uncertainty about the cause of the delay.
Keep partner-specific language memory flexible: accept new wording without global conflict while preserving the old pact and its relationship scope.
limitations: The result comes from one constrained confederate matching paradigm with simple objects and an immediate speaker change. Later eye-tracking work (doi:10.1016/j.jml.2006.05.002) argues that the partner effect may reflect late recovery from partner-independent preemption rather than early access to partner-specific common ground. Neither study establishes the duration, scale, or emotional consequences of partner-specific wording in natural messaging.

search_queries: site:sciencedirect.com "When conceptual pacts are broken"
Metzing Brennan 2003 partner-specific DOI

title: Language style matching predicts relationship initiation and stability
research_question: Does similarity in romantic partners' function-word use predict mutual interest and later relationship stability?
method: Two natural-language observational studies computed language style matching in speed-date transcripts and couples' instant messages, then related it to mutual interest and a three-month follow-up.
population_or_data: Forty speed-date conversations and instant-message records from 86 dating couples, with a three-month stability follow-up for the couples.
core_finding: Dyads with higher function-word matching were more likely to report mutual interest after speed dating, and established couples with higher matching were more likely to remain together three months later.
architecture_implications: Represent language style matching as symmetric dyadic similarity over a defined window, not as directional accommodation or a stable property of either person; direction requires temporally conditioned reply analysis.
Use language matching only as weak contextual evidence about coordination; never make it a causal relationship score or a license to imitate a contact's style.
limitations: Both studies are observational and involve modest samples in romantic contexts, so matching may proxy topic, shared environment, or relationship stage. The evidence does not show that increasing matching would improve a relationship or generalize to other ties.

search_queries: site:pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov "Language style matching predicts relationship initiation and stability"
Ireland Slatcher Eastwick 2011 LSM DOI

title: Mark my words!: linguistic style accommodation in social media
venue: Proceedings of the 20th International Conference on World Wide Web
research_question: Does turn-level linguistic accommodation occur in large-scale, asynchronous social-media conversations after controlling for baseline similarity and topic?
method: A conditional probabilistic framework measured style-category reuse in replies while controlling pair-specific baselines and topical features; analyses used 15 million tweets, including 215,000 conversations among 2,200 reciprocating user pairs.
population_or_data: Public Twitter activity from 7,800 users, with the principal pair-level analysis covering 2,200 reciprocating pairs and about 420,000 conversational turns.
core_finding: Reply writers accommodated on nearly all tested non-topical style dimensions, but convergence was often asymmetric and varied by dimension; follower and followee counts were only weakly related to who exerted more stylistic influence.
architecture_implications: Estimate accommodation from temporally ordered replies against each dyad's own baseline, with controls for topic and pre-existing similarity, rather than from aggregate text resemblance.
Do not infer social status, closeness, or identity from accommodation alone; store direction, feature dimension, and uncertainty because convergence and divergence can coexist within one dyad.
limitations: The study analyzes public, early-era English Twitter with a 140-character limit and platform-specific reply reconstruction. It is observational, relies on LIWC categories, cannot establish motives, and the original data could not be released under Twitter's terms. Its metric captures instant turn-to-turn accommodation; the paper explicitly leaves long-term accommodation and change across relationship development unresolved.

title: Investigating the Sources of Linguistic Alignment in Conversation
venue: Proceedings of the 54th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers)
research_question: Is conversational alignment primarily broad category priming, lexical reuse, or a strategic response to discourse acts?
method: The authors developed a length-robust conditional alignment model and applied it to Switchboard telephone conversations and Twitter replies, comparing lexical and word-category alignment and variation across annotated discourse acts.
population_or_data: Two English conversational corpora: Switchboard telephone dialogue and a corpus of Twitter reply conversations.
core_finding: Alignment appeared mainly at the individual-word level, with limited evidence for category-level alignment, and its magnitude changed substantially across discourse acts, supporting a discourse-sensitive rather than uniformly automatic account.
architecture_implications: Condition alignment features on discourse act and lexical item; aggregate stylistic similarity can hide whether a response is acknowledging, answering, or introducing new information.
Use conditional temporal metrics and expose alternative explanations, because baseline similarity is not evidence that one partner adapted to the other and even conditional alignment is not causal.
limitations: The evidence is limited to two English corpora and depends on corpus annotations, vocabulary choices, and model assumptions. The conditional design improves temporal inference but cannot eliminate topic, selection, or unobserved social-context effects.

Doyle Frank 2016 WHAM Switchboard Twitter alignment

title: When modesty prevails: Differential favorability of self-presentation to friends and strangers
venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
research_question: Does the favorability of self-presentation change depending on whether the audience is a friend or a stranger?
method: Five studies compared spontaneous and instructed self-presentation to friends and strangers and tested memory costs when participants used a relationship-incongruent strategy.
population_or_data: Participants across five laboratory studies of friend-versus-stranger self-presentation; sample sizes are not stated in the inspected abstract.
core_finding: Participants were more modest with friends and more self-enhancing with strangers; instructed modesty with strangers and self-enhancement with friends disrupted later interaction recall, consistent with overlearned audience-specific strategies.
architecture_implications: Maintain relationship-specific self-presentation policies so modesty, self-enhancement, and disclosure choices can differ by audience without being treated as contradictions in the owner's identity.
When evidence from one audience conflicts with another, preserve both observations with relationship scope and avoid selecting the most favorable version as the global persona.
limitations: The five studies reduce relationship context largely to friend versus stranger and the inspected abstract does not provide sample composition. Cultural norms, medium, hierarchy, and relationship stage may alter which presentation is expected.

title: Managing Impressions Online: Self-Presentation Processes in the Online Dating Environment
research_question: How do online daters manage the tension between an attractive presentation, authenticity, and anticipated face-to-face verification?
method: Semi-structured telephone interviews were recorded, transcribed, and qualitatively coded to examine profile construction, disclosure, honesty, and assessment of other profiles.
population_or_data: Thirty-four active users of one large online dating service, ages 25 to 70, sampled from California and seeking other-sex relationships.
core_finding: Participants highlighted positive and sometimes idealized aspects of self while trying to remain credible enough for a later offline meeting; they used small profile cues and verification strategies to manage uncertainty about others' claims.
architecture_implications: Store factual self-claims, aspirational self-descriptions, and presentation goals as distinct fields; anticipated future verification should constrain any generated self-enhancement.
Condition self-presentation on relationship goal and stage, and attach credibility or source evidence rather than flattening profile language into settled autobiographical fact.
limitations: The qualitative sample is small, West Coast, limited to one service and other-sex partner seeking, and based on self-report. Platform conventions and dating norms from 2003 may not generalize to current private messaging or other relationships.

Ellison Heino Gibbs 2006 online dating 34 interviews DOI

title: I tweet honestly, I tweet passionately: Twitter users, context collapse, and the imagined audience
research_question: How do Twitter users imagine and manage an audience when multiple social contexts share one public channel?
method: Qualitative interviews with Twitter users who had different kinds of followings examined audience targeting, concealment, authenticity, and personal-brand practices.
population_or_data: Twitter content producers with varied follower configurations; the inspected publisher abstract does not report the participant count.
core_finding: Users acted toward an imagined networked audience and coped with collapsed contexts through tactics such as directing posts toward selected readers, hiding some topics, and performing an audience-compatible form of authenticity.
architecture_implications: Require an explicit intended-audience representation for outward communication and separately track the reachable audience; do not infer that a public-looking message expresses the same self to every relationship.
When incompatible audiences are present, support topic concealment, audience segmentation, or abstention instead of synthesizing one compromise persona that may violate every context.
limitations: The evidence is qualitative, based on early public Twitter, and the inspected abstract omits sample size. Public posting and micro-celebrity practices may differ substantially from private or small-group messaging.

search_queries: site:journals.sagepub.com "I tweet honestly, I tweet passionately"
Marwick boyd context collapse imagined audience DOI

title: The Imagined Audience on Social Network Sites
research_question: Whom do people imagine while composing individual social-network posts, and does that audience remain stable across posts?
method: A two-month mixed-methods diary study captured posts and contemporaneous audience reports, followed by semi-structured interviews with a stratified subset.
population_or_data: One hundred nineteen United States adults, about 1,200 Facebook, Twitter, or LinkedIn posts, and 30 follow-up interviews.
core_finding: Imagined audiences shifted from post to post between broad abstract audiences and specific targets; targeted audiences most often involved personal ties, even when technical privacy settings and the potential audience were unchanged.
architecture_implications: Store audience intent at message or episode level rather than assigning the owner one permanent imagined audience for a platform or thread.
Separate intended, imagined, permitted, and observed audiences; use mismatch among them as a reason to ask for confirmation before sensitive generation or disclosure.
limitations: The sample was deliberately diverse but nonrepresentative, relied partly on self-report, and was dominated by Facebook posts collected in 2014. The study measures imagined rather than actual audiences.

search_queries: site:journals.sagepub.com "The Imagined Audience on Social Network Sites"
Litt Hargittai 2016 diary 119 1200 posts

title: Quantifying the Invisible Audience in Social Networks
venue: Proceedings of the SIGCHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems
research_question: How accurately do social-network users estimate who sees their posts, and can visible feedback or friend count support reliable estimates?
method: Two surveys elicited general and post-specific audience estimates and explanations, then compared them with server-side exposure logs; a larger log analysis tested possible visible audience heuristics.
population_or_data: 542 respondents to a general-audience survey, 589 to a specific-post survey, and one month of friends-only post exposure logs from about 222,000 United States Facebook users.
core_finding: Users usually underestimated their audience by several-fold, and visible cues such as friend count, likes, comments, and prior reach were too variable to estimate a post's actual audience reliably.
architecture_implications: Do not use engagement feedback as a proxy for who received or remembers a message; maintain explicit delivery and visibility uncertainty and expose it before audience-sensitive actions.
Treat the owner's inferred audience model as fallible evidence, and prefer current permission or reach data over assumptions learned from prior posting behavior.
limitations: The data come from United States Facebook users with friends-only posts in 2012. The exposure event indicates viewport display rather than attention, the study does not identify audience composition, and platform ranking has changed.

search_queries: "Quantifying the invisible audience in social networks" DOI
Bernstein Bakshy Burke Karrer 2013 Facebook 222000 audience

title: The Impact of Context Collapse and Privacy on Social Network Site Disclosures
research_question: How do audience composition, privacy concerns, and privacy-setting strategies relate to disclosure and social capital on social network sites?
method: A quantitative model related network size and diversity, disclosure behavior, privacy concerns and tools, and bridging social capital using survey data.
population_or_data: Social-network-site users surveyed about audiences, disclosures, privacy strategies, and social capital; sample size is not stated in the inspected publisher abstract.
core_finding: Audience size and diversity were associated with disclosure choices and use of advanced privacy settings; privacy concern and settings had different relationships with disclosure, while audience and disclosure characteristics predicted bridging social capital.
architecture_implications: Represent disclosure policy as a matrix over information type, audience member or group, and channel visibility; a large mixed audience should trigger an audience check or segmentation option, not an assumption that the owner will disclose less.
Do not treat privacy-tool use or stated concern as a single latent privacy trait: keep each rule and behavior scoped to the audience and information category it governed.
limitations: The inspected abstract does not report sample size, and the survey design is correlational and self-reported. Results reflect one social-network era and cannot establish that audience diversity or privacy settings caused disclosure changes. A preregistered close replication (doi:10.1093/joc/jqaf007) found the audience-composition paths replicated with small effects, but the privacy-concern-to-disclosure relationship did not replicate and reversed direction.

title: Public Intimacy: Disclosure Interpretation and Social Judgments on Facebook
research_question: How does a public versus private Facebook context change perceived intimacy, appropriateness, and liking after a personal disclosure?
method: Two experiments manipulated disclosure visibility and intimacy, then measured message and relational intimacy, appropriateness judgments, and liking for the discloser.
population_or_data: Perceivers in two experiments evaluating Facebook-style disclosures; sample sizes are not reported in the inspected publisher abstract.
core_finding: Private disclosures were interpreted as more personally and relationally intimate, while intimate information posted publicly was judged less appropriate; reduced appropriateness accounted for lower liking of the public discloser.
architecture_implications: Condition disclosure generation on both relationship and channel visibility; the same content can carry different intimacy and appropriateness meanings in a private chat versus a shared space.
Before generating sensitive content, estimate not only whether the owner has disclosed it before but whether the prior disclosure occurred to the same audience under comparable privacy conditions.
limitations: The studies use experimentally constructed Facebook contexts and perceiver judgments rather than observed relationship outcomes. Sample details are absent from the inspected abstract, and platform norms may have changed.

Bazarova 2012 Facebook public private disclosure DOI

title: We're in it together: interpersonal management of disclosure in social network services
venue: Proceedings of the SIGCHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems
research_question: How do people manage disclosures that affect both themselves and others when social-network privacy control is distributed across relationships?
method: Qualitative analysis of 24 individual interviews and five focus groups, using open coding to identify boundary concerns and preventive, corrective, individual, and collaborative strategies.
population_or_data: Twenty-seven university students in Finland, including domestic technology students and international graduate art and design students who used Facebook and other social-network services.
core_finding: Participants relied on both individual and collaborative boundary work, including audience zones, private channels, negotiated rules, approval requests, untagging, and removal requests; unspoken expectations left them vulnerable to accidental disclosures by others.
architecture_implications: Treat information about another person as co-owned: attach subject, discloser, audience, and consent state, and require approval or conservative redaction when third-party boundaries are unknown.

Support preventive as well as corrective rules, including audience segmentation, pre-disclosure confirmation, and revocable relationship-scoped permissions rather than relying on deletion after exposure.
limitations: The exploratory sample contains 27 students from one higher-education setting in Finland and cannot establish prevalence. Practices reflect 2009-2010 social-network affordances and self-reported experiences.

search_queries: "We're in it together" Lampinen Lehtinen Lehmuskallio Tamminen DOI

title: Imagined Communities: Awareness, Information Sharing, and Privacy on the Facebook
research_question: How do privacy attitudes, network participation, actual profile disclosure, and awareness of audience size and visibility relate on Facebook?
method: A representative institutional survey was compared with information retrieved from Facebook profiles, including analysis of membership, disclosure, privacy concern, and reactions to privacy-related exposure.
population_or_data: Members and nonmembers of Facebook at one United States academic institution; sample size is not stated in the inspected publisher abstract.
core_finding: Privacy concern weakly predicted Facebook membership, and concerned users still revealed substantial information; participants also held mistaken beliefs about community size, composition, and profile visibility.
architecture_implications: Never infer consent or audience permission from a history of disclosure: require explicit current policy because behavior can diverge from stated concern and from an accurate understanding of visibility.
Record the user's visibility assumptions separately from actual access controls and surface mismatches before using or sharing relationship-sensitive information.
limitations: The study covers early Facebook at one United States academic institution, before major changes in platform population and privacy controls. The design documents attitude-behavior gaps but does not identify a single causal mechanism.
